phrasalVerbs.definition: To appear or happen suddenly or unexpectedly.
examples: Unexpected problems cropped up during the renovation.
Opportunities tend to crop up when you least expect them.
New evidence has cropped up that changes everything.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be the cause of someone's physical, emotional, or mental problems; to make mistakes or ruin something.
examples: The divorce really messed her up for years.
I completely messed up the presentation.
Don't mess up this opportunity—it's too important.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If something piles up, the amount of it increases a lot; to accumulate.
examples: Work has been piling up while I was on holiday.
The evidence against him continues to pile up.
Bills can pile up quickly if you ignore them.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To cause difficulties or pain for someone; if children play up, they behave badly.
examples: My back has been playing up again recently.
The car's engine has been playing up for weeks.
The children always play up when we have guests.
I slipped up and sent the email to the wrong person.
Everyone slips up occasionally—don't be too hard on yourself.
The accountant slipped up with the calculations.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If you are snowed under, you have too much of something to deal with.
examples: I'm completely snowed under with work at the moment.
The customer service team was snowed under with complaints.
She was snowed under with applications for the position.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To do something that will cause problems in the future; to keep a lot of something.
examples: Avoiding the issue now will only store up problems for later.
She's been storing up resentment for years.
They stored up supplies for the winter.

fillInExercises.sentence: Several unexpected issues ___ during the project.
Don't ___ up this chance—it might be your last one.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Mess up' means to ruin or spoil something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Unread emails have been ___ in my inbox all week.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pile up' means to accumulate in large quantities.

fillInExercises.sentence: My old computer has been ___ up lately.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Play up' means to cause problems or difficulties.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ and revealed the surprise party by accident.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Slip up' means to make a careless mistake.

fillInExercises.sentence: The department is ___ under with year-end reports.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Snowed under' means to be overwhelmed with work.

fillInExercises.sentence: By ignoring the warnings, they're ___ trouble for the future.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Store up' means to create future problems.

fillInExercises.sentence: New challenges keep ___ up every day.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Crop up' means to appear or occur unexpectedly.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I'm completely [snow under] with deadlines this week.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: I know what you mean. Tasks keep [pile up] on my desk too.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: A technical issue [crop up] this morning that delayed everything.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: The printer has been [play up] as well—terrible timing!

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I [slip up] on a client email yesterday too.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: We're [store up] problems by taking on too much work.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: The kids have been [play up] all weekend.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: And domestic chores have been [pile up] as a result.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Something always seems to [crop up] when we make plans.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: I [mess up] the booking for the restaurant too.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: We can't keep [store up] these household tasks forever though.
